Interactive data dictionary

Double LASSO for Causal Inference: Does Abortion Reduce Crime?

The Belloni–Chernozhukov–Hansen Levitt panel — 48 U.S. states × 12 first-differenced years, 284 candidate controls.

datasets

U.S. states

Downloads

Each dataset is available as a labeled Stata .dta and its source file.

⇩ Download all data (ZIP)stata_codebook.do

DatasetGrainRowsStataSource
levitt_statestate-year (one row per observation)576 × 1levitt_state.dtalevitt_state.csv
levitt_linearstate-year (first-differenced)576 × 7levitt_linear.dtalevitt_linear.csv
levitt_partialledstate-year (partialled)576 × 7levitt_partialled.dtalevitt_partialled.csv
levitt_controls_violstate-year x control576 × 284levitt_controls_viol.dtalevitt_controls_viol.csv
levitt_controls_propstate-year x control576 × 284levitt_controls_prop.dtalevitt_controls_prop.csv
levitt_controls_murdstate-year x control576 × 284levitt_controls_murd.dtalevitt_controls_murd.csv
levitt_control_namescontrol index (1-284)284 × 4levitt_control_names.dtalevitt_control_names.csv

Run stata_codebook.do in Stata once to attach long-form per-variable notes to the .dta files.

Load directly in code

Every file loads straight from GitHub (raw URLs). Swap the file name to load any dataset.

Stata

* Stata 14+ : `use` reads an https URL directly
global BASE "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cmg777/starter-academic-v501/master/content/post/r_double_lasso/data/"
use "${BASE}levitt_state.dta", clear
describe
notes

Python

!pip install -q pyreadstat
import pandas as pd
BASE = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cmg777/starter-academic-v501/master/content/post/r_double_lasso/data/"
df = pd.read_stata(BASE + "levitt_state.dta")

# load every dataset at once
files = ["levitt_state", "levitt_linear", "levitt_partialled", "levitt_controls_viol", "levitt_controls_prop", "levitt_controls_murd", "levitt_control_names"]
data = {f: pd.read_stata(BASE + f + ".dta") for f in files}

# pyreadstat (richest metadata) reads LOCAL files -> download first
import pyreadstat, urllib.request
urllib.request.urlretrieve(BASE + "levitt_state.dta", "levitt_state.dta")
df, meta = pyreadstat.read_dta("levitt_state.dta")

Copy and paste this snippet in Google Colab app. https://colab.research.google.com/notebooks/empty.ipynb

R

# R : haven::read_dta auto-downloads an https URL
library(haven)
BASE <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cmg777/starter-academic-v501/master/content/post/r_double_lasso/data/"
df <- read_dta(paste0(BASE, "levitt_state.dta"))

Overview & sources

Companion data for a hands-on R tutorial that replicates the empirical example in Fitzgerald, Lattimore, Robinson & Zhu (2026, Journal of Applied Econometrics), which itself builds on Belloni, Chernozhukov & Hansen (2014) and the controversial Donohue & Levitt (2001) finding that legalized abortion reduced U.S. crime. The panel is 48 U.S. states over 12 first-differenced years (1986–1997, 576 observations) after the raw 13-year (1985–1997) panel is first-differenced to absorb state fixed effects. The treatment is the effective abortion rate; the three outcomes are violent crime, property crime, and murder rates; and the candidate-control set has 284 columns per outcome. The post compares five estimators — first-difference OLS, kitchen-sink OLS, Post-Structural LASSO, and Double LASSO under both the rigorous (hdm::rlasso) and cross-validated (glmnet::cv.glmnet) penalties — each with HC1 state-clustered standard errors. These CSVs are the inputs the post's analysis.R loads over HTTPS; they were extracted from the paper's Matlab replication archive by the companion prepare_data.R.

Seven files. levitt_state holds the state cluster id (1–48, each appearing 12 times). levitt_linear is the raw first differences of the three outcomes and treatment; levitt_partialled is the same six series after year fixed-effect partialling (so LASSO sees no time dummies). levitt_controls_viol, levitt_controls_prop, and levitt_controls_murd are the three 576×284 candidate-control matrices (one per outcome equation), already partialled and multicollinearity-screened. levitt_control_names is the 284-row crosswalk mapping each column index to its variable name in each of the three control matrices.

Data sources

SourceProvidesReference / URL
Fitzgerald et al. (2026)Replicated study; the Matlab replication archive these CSVs were extracted fromFitzgerald Sice, J., Lattimore, F., Robinson, T. & Zhu, A. (2026). Double LASSO: Replication and Practical Insights. Journal of Applied Econometrics, forthcoming. https://doi.org/10.15456/jae.2025335.0258270663
Belloni, Chernozhukov &amp; Hansen (2014)Double LASSO method; the 284-control extension of the Levitt panel used hereBelloni, A., Chernozhukov, V. & Hansen, C. (2014). Inference on treatment effects after selection among high-dimensional controls. Review of Economic Studies, 81(2), 608–650. https://doi.org/10.1093/restud/rdt044
Donohue &amp; Levitt (2001)Original empirical study and the abortion–crime panel of 48 U.S. statesDonohue III, J. J. & Levitt, S. D. (2001). The impact of legalized abortion on crime. Quarterly Journal of Economics, 116(2), 379–420. https://doi.org/10.1162/00335530151144050
Method referencesRigorous LASSO penalty and cluster-robust inferenceBelloni, Chen, Chernozhukov & Hansen (2012); Tibshirani (1996); Cameron & Miller (2015).

Cite this data

Please cite this dataset as follows.

APA

Mendez, C. (2026). Double LASSO for Causal Inference: Does Abortion Reduce Crime? [Data set]. https://carlos-mendez.org/post/r_double_lasso/

Belloni, A., Chernozhukov, V. & Hansen, C. (2014). Inference on treatment effects after selection among high-dimensional controls. Review of Economic Studies, 81(2), 608–650. Donohue III, J. J. & Levitt, S. D. (2001). The impact of legalized abortion on crime. Quarterly Journal of Economics, 116(2), 379–420.

BibTeX

@misc{mendez2026rdoublelasso,
  author       = {Mendez, Carlos},
  title        = {Double LASSO for Causal Inference: Does Abortion Reduce Crime?},
  year         = {2026},
  howpublished = {\url{https://carlos-mendez.org/post/r_double_lasso/}},
  note         = {Data set}
}

@article{belloni2014inference,
  author  = {Belloni, Alexandre and Chernozhukov, Victor and Hansen, Christian},
  title   = {Inference on Treatment Effects after Selection among High-Dimensional Controls},
  journal = {Review of Economic Studies},
  volume  = {81}, number = {2}, pages = {608--650}, year = {2014}
}
@article{donohue2001impact,
  author  = {Donohue, John J. and Levitt, Steven D.},
  title   = {The Impact of Legalized Abortion on Crime},
  journal = {Quarterly Journal of Economics},
  volume  = {116}, number = {2}, pages = {379--420}, year = {2001}
}

Construction & formulas

The structural equation of interest is, for state s and differenced year t, Δy_st = α·Δd_st + x_st'θ + ε_st, where Δy is a first-differenced crime rate, Δd is the differenced effective abortion rate, and the parameter of interest is the average partial effect α.

  • First differencing (levitt_linear): the raw 13-year (1985–1997) level panel is differenced to 12 years (1986–1997), absorbing all time-invariant state effects. Columns Dy* are the differenced outcomes, Dx* the differenced treatment.
  • Year-FE partialling (levitt_partialled): each differenced series is residualised against year dummies via the Frisch–Waugh–Lovell projection, so the LASSO regressions carry no time dummies. Dy*/Dx* become the partialled Dy*/Dx* (uppercase suffix V/P/M).
  • Rigorous LASSO penalty (Belloni et al. 2012): λ^rig = 2·c·σ̂/√n · Φ⁻¹(1 − γ/(2p)) with c = 1.1, γ = 0.05, n = 576, p = 284 — a Bonferroni-style, theory-driven penalty (not tuned for prediction).
  • Double LASSO: LASSO of y on X (index set I_y) and LASSO of d on X (index set I_d); refit plain OLS of y on d and the union I_y ∪ I_d. The union approximates the FWL residual-maker M_X = I − X(X'X)⁻¹X' without using all 284 controls.
  • State-clustered SE (HC1): V̂ = (n−1)/(n−k) · G/(G−1) · (X'X)⁻¹ · [Σ_g X_g'ê_g ê_g'X_g] · (X'X)⁻¹ with G = 48 states.

Control-matrix column names follow the replication archive's Matlab scheme: prefix D = first difference, L = lagged level, ^2 = square, * = interaction, t = time trend; e.g. Lprison^2 is the squared lagged prison-population level and xV0*t is an initial-value × trend interaction for the violent-crime equation. The eight base Donohue–Levitt controls are prison population, police, unemployment, income, poverty, AFDC generosity, beer consumption, and a lagged-prisoner term, expanded into 284 columns of squares, interactions, time interactions, and initial-value × trend terms.

The datasets

Switch datasets with the tabs. Each shows the full variable dictionary plus a sortable statistics table with mini distributions and data coverage.

expand to search (Ctrl/⌘+F) or print across all datasets

state-year (one row per observation)  576 × 1 · 1986-1997 (differenced) · 48 states x 12 differenced years = 576 rows

Panel key: row order matches all other files · Cluster id for the state-clustered (HC1) standard errors.

Variable dictionary

VariableLabelDefinitionConstructionUnitsSourceCoverage
state identifierState cluster identifier (1-48)Integer id of the U.S. state for an observation; the clustering variable for HC1 standard errors.From the replication archive's State.mat (Dstate); 1..48, each state appears 12 times.integer 1-48Donohue & Levitt (2001) / BCH (2014) panel576 rows

VariableDistributionCoverageNDistinctMinMeanMedianMaxSD

state-year (first-differenced)  576 × 7 · 1986-1997 (differenced) · 576 rows; 48 states x 12 years

Panel key: row order x state · First-difference OLS baseline (no controls) for each outcome.

Variable dictionary

VariableLabelDefinitionConstructionUnitsSourceCoverage
state identifierState cluster identifier (1-48)Integer id of the U.S. state for an observation; the clustering variable for HC1 standard errors.From the replication archive's State.mat (Dstate); 1..48, each state appears 12 times.integer 1-48Donohue & Levitt (2001) / BCH (2014) panel576 rows
Dyv continuousΔ violent-crime rate (raw first difference)First difference of the violent-crime rate (per 100,000, log-change scale).Year-over-year difference of the level violent-crime series, 1986-1997.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dxv continuousΔ effective abortion rate (violent-crime sample)First difference of the effective abortion rate aligned to the violent-crime equation.Year-over-year difference of the effective abortion rate (lagged abortion-to-birth weighted average).differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dyp continuousΔ property-crime rate (raw first difference)First difference of the property-crime rate (per 100,000, log-change scale).Year-over-year difference of the level property-crime series, 1986-1997.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dxp continuousΔ effective abortion rate (property-crime sample)First difference of the effective abortion rate aligned to the property-crime equation.Year-over-year difference of the effective abortion rate.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dym continuousΔ murder rate (raw first difference)First difference of the murder rate (per 100,000, log-change scale).Year-over-year difference of the level murder series, 1986-1997.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dxm continuousΔ effective abortion rate (murder sample)First difference of the effective abortion rate aligned to the murder equation.Year-over-year difference of the effective abortion rate.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel

Variable dictionary

VariableLabelDefinitionConstructionUnitsSourceCoverage
state identifierState cluster identifier (1-48)Integer id of the U.S. state for an observation; the clustering variable for HC1 standard errors.From the replication archive's State.mat (Dstate); 1..48, each state appears 12 times.integer 1-48Donohue & Levitt (2001) / BCH (2014) panel576 rows
DxV continuousPartialled Δ abortion rate (violent equation)Differenced effective abortion rate after year fixed-effect partialling, violent-crime equation.Residual of Dxv on year dummies (Frisch-Waugh-Lovell).partialled differenced rateComputed (this study, from BCH panel)
DyV continuousPartialled Δ violent-crime rateDifferenced violent-crime rate after year fixed-effect partialling.Residual of Dyv on year dummies (Frisch-Waugh-Lovell).partialled differenced rateComputed (this study, from BCH panel)
DxP continuousPartialled Δ abortion rate (property equation)Differenced effective abortion rate after year fixed-effect partialling, property-crime equation.Residual of Dxp on year dummies (Frisch-Waugh-Lovell).partialled differenced rateComputed (this study, from BCH panel)
DyP continuousPartialled Δ property-crime rateDifferenced property-crime rate after year fixed-effect partialling.Residual of Dyp on year dummies (Frisch-Waugh-Lovell).partialled differenced rateComputed (this study, from BCH panel)
DxM continuousPartialled Δ abortion rate (murder equation)Differenced effective abortion rate after year fixed-effect partialling, murder equation.Residual of Dxm on year dummies (Frisch-Waugh-Lovell).partialled differenced rateComputed (this study, from BCH panel)
DyM continuousPartialled Δ murder rateDifferenced murder rate after year fixed-effect partialling.Residual of Dym on year dummies (Frisch-Waugh-Lovell).partialled differenced rateComputed (this study, from BCH panel)

state-year x control  576 × 284 · 1986-1997 (differenced) · 576 rows x 284 controls

Panel key: row order x state; columns = named controls · The 284 candidate controls the LASSO selects from for violent crime.

Variable dictionary

VariableLabelDefinitionConstructionUnitsSourceCoverage
Dprison continuousΔ prison population per capitaFirst difference of the prisoners-per-capita control (one of the 8 base Donohue-Levitt controls).Year-over-year difference of prisoners per capita.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dpolice continuousΔ police per capitaFirst difference of the police-per-capita control.Year-over-year difference of police per capita.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dur continuousΔ unemployment rateFirst difference of the state unemployment rate control.Year-over-year difference of the unemployment rate.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dinc continuousΔ income per capitaFirst difference of the income-per-capita control.Year-over-year difference of income per capita.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dpov continuousΔ poverty rateFirst difference of the poverty-rate control.Year-over-year difference of the poverty rate.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dafdc continuousΔ AFDC generosityFirst difference of the AFDC (welfare) generosity control.Year-over-year difference of AFDC generosity per recipient.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dbeer continuousΔ beer consumption per capitaFirst difference of the beer-consumption control.Year-over-year difference of beer consumption per capita.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel

state-year x control  576 × 284 · 1986-1997 (differenced) · 576 rows x 284 controls

Panel key: row order x state; columns = named controls · The 284 candidate controls the LASSO selects from for property crime.

Variable dictionary

VariableLabelDefinitionConstructionUnitsSourceCoverage
Dprison continuousΔ prison population per capitaFirst difference of the prisoners-per-capita control (one of the 8 base Donohue-Levitt controls).Year-over-year difference of prisoners per capita.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dpolice continuousΔ police per capitaFirst difference of the police-per-capita control.Year-over-year difference of police per capita.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dur continuousΔ unemployment rateFirst difference of the state unemployment rate control.Year-over-year difference of the unemployment rate.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dinc continuousΔ income per capitaFirst difference of the income-per-capita control.Year-over-year difference of income per capita.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dpov continuousΔ poverty rateFirst difference of the poverty-rate control.Year-over-year difference of the poverty rate.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dafdc continuousΔ AFDC generosityFirst difference of the AFDC (welfare) generosity control.Year-over-year difference of AFDC generosity per recipient.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dbeer continuousΔ beer consumption per capitaFirst difference of the beer-consumption control.Year-over-year difference of beer consumption per capita.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel

state-year x control  576 × 284 · 1986-1997 (differenced) · 576 rows x 284 controls

Panel key: row order x state; columns = named controls · The 284 candidate controls the LASSO selects from for murder.

Variable dictionary

VariableLabelDefinitionConstructionUnitsSourceCoverage
Dprison continuousΔ prison population per capitaFirst difference of the prisoners-per-capita control (one of the 8 base Donohue-Levitt controls).Year-over-year difference of prisoners per capita.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dpolice continuousΔ police per capitaFirst difference of the police-per-capita control.Year-over-year difference of police per capita.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dur continuousΔ unemployment rateFirst difference of the state unemployment rate control.Year-over-year difference of the unemployment rate.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dinc continuousΔ income per capitaFirst difference of the income-per-capita control.Year-over-year difference of income per capita.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dpov continuousΔ poverty rateFirst difference of the poverty-rate control.Year-over-year difference of the poverty rate.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dafdc continuousΔ AFDC generosityFirst difference of the AFDC (welfare) generosity control.Year-over-year difference of AFDC generosity per recipient.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel
Dbeer continuousΔ beer consumption per capitaFirst difference of the beer-consumption control.Year-over-year difference of beer consumption per capita.differenced rateDonohue & Levitt panel

control index (1-284)  284 × 4 · n/a · 284 rows x 4 columns

Panel key: index · Map each control-matrix column position to its variable name per outcome.

Variable dictionary

VariableLabelDefinitionConstructionUnitsSourceCoverage
index identifierControl-matrix column index (1-284)Sequential position of a column in each 284-column control matrix.1..284.integerThis study (crosswalk)
viol identifierControl name in the violent-crime matrixVariable name of the corresponding column in levitt_controls_viol.Matlab control name (D/L prefix, ^2 square, * interaction, t trend).stringBCH (2014) control expansion
prop identifierControl name in the property-crime matrixVariable name of the corresponding column in levitt_controls_prop.Matlab control name (D/L prefix, ^2 square, * interaction, t trend).stringBCH (2014) control expansion
murd identifierControl name in the murder matrixVariable name of the corresponding column in levitt_controls_murd.Matlab control name (D/L prefix, ^2 square, * interaction, t trend).stringBCH (2014) control expansion

VariableDistributionCoverageNDistinctMinMeanMedianMaxSD
index–100%284284—————
viol–100%284284—————
prop–100%284284—————
murd–100%284284—————

Known limitations & caveats

  • Replication, not a primary causal claim. Fitzgerald et al. (2026) is itself a methods replication; whether abortion access caused less crime is a substantive debate that goes well beyond any single regression. The data is borrowed as a clean case study of Double LASSO, not to endorse the Donohue–Levitt 2001 claim.
  • Identification rests on two assumptions. Conditional independence given the 284 partialled controls, and parallel trends in levels (state FE absorbed by first-differencing, year FE by the partialling step). Neither is innocuous.
  • Non-syntactic column names. The control matrices keep the original Matlab names (containing ^ and *), so the post loads them with check.names = FALSE. The labeled Stata .dta in this bundle sanitizes those characters to underscores (Lprison^2 → Lprison_2) so the names are valid Stata identifiers; the source CSVs keep the originals.
  • State-clustering relies on G ≥ 30. Cluster-robust inference is justified asymptotically in the number of clusters; with G = 48 states it is above the rule of thumb.
  • CV LASSO is non-deterministic. cv.glmnet randomly partitions folds; the post sets set.seed(20260520) so its numbers reproduce. The rigorous LASSO is deterministic given the data and penalty arguments.
  • Equal weighting. Every state-year observation gets equal weight; the estimand is not population-weighted.
Generated by build_data_dictionary.py · Stata format .dta v118 · Back to the post
